M02 MKT 472 · Marketing Metrics and Marketing Profitability
01 / 20
Learning Objectives
After reading Ch. 2 and viewing this deck, you should be able to:
1
Distinguish financial (internal) from marketing (external) performance metrics
2
Identify and classify the four categories of marketing performance metrics
3
Understand the forward vs. backward-looking metric distinction
4
Calculate NMC and explain how it differs from net profit
5
Calculate marketing ROS and marketing ROI; use them to benchmark performance
6
Use NMC decomposition to plan and evaluate marketing strategies

LO1 BioTronics — Financial Performance
02 / 20
BioTronics: The Financial Picture
Three years of impressive growth — or is it?
Stop & Think

Before the next slide: based only on this data, would you say BioTronics is performing well? What would you recommend to management?

What financial metrics show

Sales +54% over 3 years. Pre-tax profit more than tripled. All three financial metrics improved each year.

What financial metrics cannot show

Market share trajectory · Competitive position · Customer satisfaction trend · Customer lifetime value

LO1
Fig 2-1 and 2-3 in Ch. 2
LO1 BioTronics — Marketing Performance
03 / 20
BioTronics: The Marketing Picture
Adding marketing metrics reveals a very different story
Insight 1 — Market

Market share fell 10.0% → 8.5% — undetected because market grew faster than BioTronics.

Insight 2 — Competitive

Product performance and service quality declined relative to competitors each year.

Insight 3 — Customer

CSI fell 78→64. Retention fell 77%→65%. CLV collapsed from $103 to $45. The customer base eroded.

LO1
Fig 2-2 in Ch. 2 — Gate 1 Q5
LO1 The Cost of Ignoring Marketing Metrics
04 / 20
The $41M That Got Away
What would have happened if BioTronics maintained its 10% market share?
PerformanceBase YearYear 1Year 2Year 3
Pre-Tax Profit — Actual Strategy$16M$29M$39M$55M
Pre-Tax Profit — Held 10% Share$16M$34M$52M$78M
Lost Profit$0−$5M−$13M−$23M
Total forgone profit: $41 million over 3 years — the direct cost of managing by financial metrics alone
Chapter-exclusive (Fig 2-4)

The held-share strategy would have delivered 17% pre-tax ROS vs. 14.1% achieved. Required additional investment in marketing, sales, and R&D to keep pace with 21% annual market growth. Gate 1 Q5 target.

LO1
Fig 2-4 — Chapter-exclusive — Gate 1 Q5
LO2 Marketing Metric Categories
05 / 20
Four Categories of Marketing Metrics
Complementing financial metrics with an external view of performance

Market Metrics

Measure market size, growth, and competitive position
• Market growth rate
• Market share
• Relative market share
• Market development index

Customer Metrics

Gauge performance with customers over time
• Customer satisfaction (CSI)
• Customer retention rate
• Customer lifetime value (CLV)
• Revenue per customer

Competitiveness Metrics

Index the business against benchmark competitors
• Relative product performance
• Relative service quality
• Customer value index
• Brand image

Marketing Profitability Metrics

Measure profit impact of marketing strategies
• Net Marketing Contribution (NMC)
• Marketing Return on Sales (ROS)
• Marketing Return on Investment (ROI)
LO2
Fig 2-6 — Chapter-exclusive — Gate 1 Q1
LO2 Metric Classification Framework
06 / 20
Internal vs. External × Forward vs. Backward
Every performance metric occupies one cell — Ch. 2 Fig 2-8
Key insight

Forward-looking metrics predict future behavior. Backward-looking metrics confirm what already happened. Customer satisfaction → forward external. Net profit → backward internal.

Chapter-exclusive — Gate 1 Q2

Four criteria for good metrics (Ch. 2 p.44):
• Necessary
• Precise
• Consistent
• Sufficient

LO2LO3
Fig 2-8 — Gate 1 Q2, Q3 targets
LO2 Marketing Metrics and Profit Correlation
07 / 20
Marketing Metrics Carry the Profitability Gene
Higher metric levels correlate consistently with higher profitability — Fig 2-7
What this means

All four metric categories show the same pattern: as the metric improves, profitability rises. Marketing metrics are not just reporting tools — they predict future financial performance before it appears in income statements.

Connection to M01

An 8-point CSI improvement (M01 module insight) appears here as Customer Retention → Profitability. Higher satisfaction → higher retention → higher profit.

LO1LO2
Fig 2-7 in Ch. 2
LO3 Net Marketing Contribution
08 / 20
Net Marketing Contribution — What and Why
Separating marketing's contribution from general overhead
TRADITIONAL VIEW
Sales Revenues$125.0
Cost of Goods Sold$76.5
Gross Profit$48.5
Marketing & Sales Expenses—
Net Marketing Contribution—
SGA & Other Expenses$38.5
Operating Income$10.0
WITH NET MARKETING CONTRIBUTION
Sales Revenues$125.0
Cost of Goods Sold$76.5
Gross Profit$48.5
Marketing & Sales Expenses$18.5
Net Marketing Contribution$30.0
G&A & Other Expenses$20.0
Operating Income$10.0

Operating income is identical ($10M) in both views. NMC ($30M) reveals how much of that $10M originated from marketing. The traditional view conceals it inside $38.5M of undifferentiated operating expenses.

LO3
Gate 2 Q1 target
LO3 NMC Formula and Calculation
09 / 20
NMC — Formula and Worked Example
Santa Fe Sportswear · Sales $125M · %Margin 38.8% · MSE $18.5M
Step 1 — Gross Profit

Sales × % Margin = $125M × 38.8% = $48.5M

Step 2 — Subtract MSE

$48.5M − $18.5M = NMC = $30.0M

Step 3 — Operating Income

$30.0M − $5.0M G&A − $15.0M Other = $10.0M

Key point

NMC does not change operating income — it reveals marketing's share of that income. $30M NMC is the only source of positive profit in the business.

LO3
Gate 2 Q1 target
LO3 Estimating Marketing & Sales Expenses
10 / 20
The 75% Rule for Estimating MSE
When companies do not report marketing and sales expenses separately
The rule

Marketing & Sales Expenses ≈ 75% of SGA Expenses
Range across 7 companies: 66–81%
Average: 74% → use 75%

When to use it

Only when MSE is not separately reported. If actual MSE is known, always use it. The 75% estimate introduces approximation error — Gate 1 Q2 target.

MSE includes

Marketing admin, advertising, sales, customer service, product management, technical support — everything directly related to bringing products to market.

LO3
Fig 2-12 — Chapter-exclusive — Gate 1 Q2
LO4 Marketing Profitability Ratio Metrics
11 / 20
Marketing ROS and Marketing ROI
Standardized ratio metrics — compare across product lines, companies, and time
24.0%
Marketing ROS
$30M ÷ $125M
162%
Marketing ROI
$30M ÷ $18.5M
$1.62
NMC per $1 MSE
Why ratio metrics matter

Company X: NMC $30M on $185M sales → ROS 16.2%
Company Y: NMC $12M on $50M sales → ROS 24.0%
Y has higher marketing profitability despite lower NMC.

ROI below 100% warning

Marketing ROI < 100% means the company earns less than $1.00 in NMC for every $1.00 spent on marketing and sales. The marketing strategy is not covering its own cost.

LO4
Gate 2 Q2 and Q3 targets
LO5 NMC Decomposition for Strategy
12 / 20
NMC Decomposition — The Strategy Formula
Each component is a lever — changing any one produces a different NMC outcome
Six strategy levers

Demand — grow the market
Share — penetrate deeper
Price — increase perceived value
Channel — reduce distribution cost
Margin — lower variable costs
MSE — improve marketing efficiency

Khaki Pants (Fig 2-16)

12M × 12.5% × $80 × 50% × 40% − $8.5M = $15.5M NMC

LO5
Gate 2 Q4 target
LO5 Product Line NMC Analysis
13 / 20
The Casual Shorts Insight
Why dropping a product with negative net profit often makes things worse
Finance manager says

Casual shorts loses $1M. Drop it.

Marketing manager says

Casual shorts has positive NMC of $1M. Dropping removes that contribution — but $2M in fixed operating expenses stays. Net profit falls from $10M to $9M.

The rule

Drop a product only when its NMC is zero or negative — not when its net profit is negative.

LO5LO6
Fig 2-14 in Ch. 2 — Gate 1 Q4
LO5 Customer Segment vs. Product Line NMC
14 / 20
Customer Segment NMC vs. Product Line NMC
Same company, same financials — very different strategic picture
Santa Fe SportswearTraditional BuyerFashion SeekerTrend SetterCompany Total
Market Demand (customers)7,000,0005,890,0008,000,00020,890,000
Market Share9.0%3.5%6.0%6.3%
Customer Volume630,000206,150480,0001,316,150
Avg. Revenue / Customer$180$360$130$190
Net Selling Price$90$180$65$95
Sales Revenues$56.7M$37.1M$31.2M$125.0M
% Margin40%35.9%40%38.8%
Gross Profit$22.7M$13.3M$12.5M$48.5M
Marketing & Sales Exp.$7.0M$6.5M$5.0M$18.5M
Net Marketing Contribution$15.7M$6.8M$7.5M$30.0M
Net Profit (before taxes)$7.7M$0.3M$2.0M$10.0M
Marketing ROI224%105%150%162%
Chapter-exclusive insight (Fig 2-18)

All three customer segments produce positive NMC and positive net profit. The product line view showed two lines with negative net profit — but those losses exist because of fixed cost allocation, not because marketing strategies failed. Both views have strategic value. Gate 1 Q4 target.

LO5
Fig 2-18 — Chapter-exclusive — Gate 1 Q4
LO5 NMC Strategy — Market Demand Growth
15 / 20
NMC Strategy: Grow Market Demand
Traditional Buyer segment · Santa Fe Sportswear
Input / OutputCurrentProposed (+1M demand)
Market Demand (customers)7,000,0008,000,000
Market Share9%9%
Avg. Revenue / Customer$180$180
% Margin40%40%
Marketing Budget$7.0M$8.0M (+$1M)
Net Sales$56.7M$64.8M
Net Marketing Contribution$15.7M$17.9M ↑
Why it works

Additional 90,000 customers × $180 × 50% × 40% = $3.24M gross. Minus $1M additional marketing = +$2.2M NMC increase. Incremental NMC from new customers exceeds incremental spend.

The rule

A demand strategy succeeds when incremental NMC from new customers exceeds incremental MSE. If market share falls as demand grows, the math changes.

LO5
Gate 2 Q4 target
LO5 NMC Strategy Comparison
16 / 20
Which Strategy to Grow Market Share?
Two strategies — only one actually works

Option A — Lower Price 10%

CurrentProposed
Market Share9%→ 11%
Price$180$162 (−10%)
% Margin40%33.3%
Net Sales$56.7M$63.9M
NMC$15.7M$14.3M ↓
FAILS — NMC drops $1.4M

Option B — Increase Advertising

CurrentProposed
Market Share9%→ 10%
Price$180$180
% Margin40%40%
Marketing Budget$7.0M$9.0M (+$2M ad)
NMC$15.7M$16.2M ↑
WORKS — NMC rises $0.5M
Why Option A fails

A 10% price cut collapses margin from 40%→33.3%. The 2-point share gain cannot offset the margin loss. Price reductions are rarely the best path to improved NMC.

LO5
Gate 2 Q4 target
LO5 Break-Even Market Share
17 / 20
Break-Even Market Share Formula
Minimum share needed to maintain NMC after a price or budget change
Method 1 — Break-even unit volume

For a 10% price reduction with 35% CM:
BE% = −(−10%) ÷ (35% + (−10%)) = 40%
New MS = 8% + (40% × 2M ÷ 25M) = 11.2%

Method 2 — Solve NMC equation for MS%

Set NMC = current NMC, solve for MS%:
$15.7M = MS% × $252M − $9M
MS% = $24.7M ÷ $252M = 9.8%

Strategic implication

The break-even share is the minimum you must achieve for the strategy to pay off. Any strategy requiring an unlikely share gain to break even is high-risk — even if it looks mathematically viable. Always ask: is that required share gain realistic?

LO5
Gate 2 Q4 target
LO6 Market Size and Market Share Growth
18 / 20
Market Size Growth Formula
How to calculate future market size when growth rate is known
Mt = Market size in period t  |  M0 = Current market size  |  g = Growth rate per period  |  t = Number of periods
Worked example

10% share of $100M market, growing at 20%:
Year 0: $100M × 1.20⁰ = $100M → Company: $10M
Year 1: $100M × 1.20¹ = $120M → Company: $12M
Year 3: $100M × 1.20³ = $172M → Company: $17.2M
Year 4: $100M × 1.20⁴ = $207M → Company: $20.7M
Sales double in ~4 years at 10% share, 20% growth.

The BioTronics lesson

BioTronics grew sales 54% over 3 years. But the biotech market grew ~21%/year — market nearly doubled. Growing slower than the market = losing share, even if revenue rises. This formula makes the gap visible.

Gate 2 Q5 — numeric

Given M0, growth rate g, and target year t — calculate future market size. Then: company sales = Mt × market share %.

LO6
Gate 2 Q5 target
LO4 Benchmarking Marketing Profitability
19 / 20
Benchmarking NMC — Apple and Fortune 500
Marketing profitability correlates with operating income — Ch. 2
Company% MarginMSE % SalesNMCMktg ROSMktg ROIOper. Inc %
Apple (2009)36.0%8.5%$10.0B27.4%323%21.0%
IBM44.1%17.0%$28.1B27.1%160%16.5%
Dell17.9%8.7%$5.6B9.2%106%5.2%
HP24.3%8.3%$18.9B16.0%192%9.1%
Toshiba25.2%16.3%$5.9B8.4%52%8.9%
F500 Average———23.5%163%10.4%
Key finding

Companies with higher marketing ROS tend to produce higher operating income as a % of sales. Apple's 323% ROI and 27.4% ROS were both highest among the five compared. This pattern holds across 169 Fortune 500 companies in Ch. 2 Fig 2-24.

Fortune 500 benchmarks (Ch. 2 Fig 2-24)

Median Marketing ROS: 19%
Average Marketing ROS: 23.5%
Median Marketing ROI: 135%
Average Marketing ROI: 163%

Apple data is 2009 — use as structural example, not current benchmark.
